summary: Jeff Atwood argues that despite Markdown's ubiquity across major platforms like GitHub, Stack Overflow, and Reddit, there's no definitive tutorial for learning it. He criticizes existing resources as merely 'OK' rather than great, and announces a contest to build an interactive, universal Markdown tutorial to be hosted on commonmark.org. The post also takes shots at WYSIWYG editors, arguing that visible formatting codes are superior for revision tracking and user control. The contest ultimately succeeded, producing the tutorial now at commonmark.org/help.
key_points: Markdown is everywhere (GitHub, Stack Overflow, Reddit) yet many people claim not to know it
WYSIWYG editors hide formatting in dangerous, invisible ways that break revision tracking and user control
All existing Markdown tutorials and references are adequate but none are truly great
Interactive, fun tutorials are more effective than static documentation
Open contests can harness community effort to build universal educational resources
money_quotes: It's always surprised me when people, especially technical people, say they don't know Markdown.
But how can we expect them to know that, if they grew up with wizzy-wig editors where the only way to make italic is to click a toolbar button, like an animal?
I personally don't like invisible formatting tags and I find that WYSIWYG is more like WYCSYCG in practice.
It's dangerous to be dependent on these invisible formatting codes you can't control.

What we really need is a great Markdown tutorial and reference page, one that we can refer anyone to, anywhere in the world, from someone who barely touches computers to the hardest of hard-core coders.
Judging will be completely at the whim of myself and John MacFarlane. Our decisions will be capricious, arbitrary, probably nonsensical, and above all, final.
Third prize is you're fired. Just kidding.
key_insight: The developer community needs a single, universally excellent interactive Markdown tutorial rather than scattered adequate documentation.
